name: Civil Rights Voices
description: Courage and conscience from the leaders who marched for freedom and equality.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: Power concedes nothing without a demand. It never did and it never will.
quotes.source: "West India Emancipation" address, Canandaigua, New York (1857)
quotes.notes: From Douglass's speech marking the anniversary of British West Indian emancipation; text preserved in the Frederick Douglass Papers.

quotes.content: If there is no struggle, there is no progress. Those who profess to favor freedom, and yet depreciate agitation, are men who want crops without plowing up the ground; they want rain without thunder and lightning.
quotes.source: "West India Emancipation" address, Canandaigua, New York (1857)
quotes.notes: Same 1857 address; among the most-cited passages of Douglass's oratory.

quotes.content: I would unite with anybody to do right; and with nobody to do wrong.
quotes.source: "The Anti-Slavery Movement" lecture, Rochester, New York (1855)
quotes.notes: Douglass's reply to the 'no union with slaveholders' abolitionist motto, arguing for pragmatic alliances.

quotes.content: I had reasoned this out in my mind; there was one of two things I had a right to, liberty or death; if I could not have one, I would have the other.
quotes.source: Sarah H. Bradford, Scenes in the Life of Harriet Tubman (1869)
quotes.notes: Tubman's words on her first escape as recorded in Bradford's biography, the earliest documented source for her recollections.

quotes.content: I freed a thousand slaves. I could have freed a thousand more, if only they had known they were slaves.
quotes.source: Attributed; no primary source
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: fact-checkers (PolitiFact, Snopes, Africa Check) find no evidence Tubman said this; the line surfaced only in the 1990s-2000s and contradicts her own accounts of freeing about 70 people.

quotes.content: I am a woman's rights. I have as much muscle as any man, and can do as much work as any man.
quotes.source: Speech to the Women's Rights Convention, Akron, Ohio; Marius Robinson transcription, Anti-Slavery Bugle (1851)
quotes.notes: From the contemporaneous 1851 transcription by Marius Robinson, whom Truth knew and who reviewed the text with her; considered the most reliable record of the speech.

quotes.content: That man over there says that women need to be helped into carriages, and lifted over ditches... Nobody ever helps me into carriages, or over mud-puddles... And ain't I a woman?
quotes.source: Frances Dana Gage's reconstruction (1863) of the 1851 Akron speech
quotes.notes: Wording axis fails: this famous 'Ain't I a woman?' version is Gage's 1863 reconstruction in a fabricated Southern dialect, published 12 years later and differing sharply from the 1851 Robinson transcript.

quotes.content: I felt that one had better die fighting against injustice than to die like a dog or a rat in a trap.
quotes.source: Crusade for Justice: The Autobiography of Ida B. Wells (1970)
quotes.notes: From her posthumously published autobiography, recounting her resolve after the 1892 Memphis lynching of her friend Thomas Moss.

quotes.content: The way to right wrongs is to turn the light of truth upon them.
quotes.source: Attributed; primary source not established
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: universally attributed to Wells and reportedly used on her lecture advertising, but not located in a dated primary text such as Southern Horrors.

quotes.content: The problem of the twentieth century is the problem of the color line.
quotes.source: The Souls of Black Folk (1903)
quotes.notes: The thesis Du Bois announces in the 'Forethought' of his landmark essay collection.

quotes.content: The cost of liberty is less than the price of repression.
quotes.source: John Brown (1909)
quotes.notes: A recurring refrain in the closing chapter, 'The Legacy of John Brown,' of Du Bois's biography of the abolitionist.

quotes.content: None of us got where we are solely by pulling ourselves up by our bootstraps. We got here because somebody—a parent, a teacher, an Ivy League crony or a few nuns—bent down and helped us pick up our boots.
quotes.source: "The Equality Speech," installation of Wiley Branton as dean of Howard Law School (1978)
quotes.notes: Delivered by the Supreme Court justice; collected in J. Clay Smith Jr., ed., Supreme Justice: Speeches and Writings (2003).

quotes.content: Until the killing of black men, black mothers' sons, becomes as important to the rest of the country as the killing of a white mother's sons, we who believe in freedom cannot rest.
quotes.source: Speech, Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party convention (1964)
quotes.notes: Spoken after the murders of James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, and Michael Schwerner; later adapted into 'Ella's Song' by Bernice Johnson Reagon.

quotes.content: Give light and people will find the way.
quotes.source: Attributed; primary source not established
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: widely cited as Baker's personal motto and used as the title of Barbara Ransby's biography, but not traced to a dated primary source.

quotes.content: We need, in every community, a group of angelic troublemakers.
quotes.source: Time on Two Crosses: The Collected Writings of Bayard Rustin
quotes.notes: Rustin's line, dated by biographers to his return from India around 1948 and echoed in later speeches; collected in his published writings.

quotes.content: The only tired I was, was tired of giving in.
quotes.source: Rosa Parks: My Story (1992)
quotes.notes: From her autobiography, correcting the myth that her refusal to give up her bus seat was merely physical exhaustion.

quotes.content: I am sick and tired of being sick and tired.
quotes.source: Speech at Williams Institutional CME Church, Harlem, New York (1964)
quotes.notes: Delivered at a rally, shared with Malcolm X, supporting the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party's congressional challenge.

quotes.content: Nobody's free until everybody's free.
quotes.source: Speech at the founding of the National Women's Political Caucus, Washington, D.C. (1971)
quotes.notes: The closing line and title of her address; collected in The Speeches of Fannie Lou Hamer: To Tell It Like It Is.

quotes.content: Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere. We are caught in an inescapable network of mutuality, tied in a single garment of destiny. Whatever affects one directly, affects all indirectly.
quotes.source: "Letter from Birmingham Jail" (1963)
quotes.notes: King's reply to clergymen who called him an 'outsider,' written from jail during the Birmingham campaign.

quotes.content: Darkness cannot drive out darkness; only light can do that. Hate cannot drive out hate; only love can do that.
quotes.source: Strength to Love, "Loving Your Enemies" (1963)
quotes.notes: From King's published sermon collection; the chapter develops his case for nonviolent, redemptive love.

quotes.content: The ultimate measure of a man is not where he stands in moments of comfort and convenience, but where he stands at times of challenge and controversy.
quotes.source: Strength to Love, "On Being a Good Neighbor" (1963)
quotes.notes: Appears in Strength to Love; an earlier version was printed in King's The Measure of a Man (1959).

quotes.content: Let us realize that the arc of the moral universe is long, but it bends toward justice.
quotes.source: "Where Do We Go from Here?" address to the SCLC, Atlanta (1967)
quotes.notes: King's paraphrase of 19th-century abolitionist Theodore Parker, whose source he acknowledged when quoting the line.

quotes.content: We want freedom by any means necessary. We want justice by any means necessary. We want equality by any means necessary.
quotes.source: Speech at the founding rally of the Organization of Afro-American Unity, Audubon Ballroom, New York (1964)
quotes.notes: Collected in By Any Means Necessary: Speeches, Interviews, and a Letter by Malcolm X (1970).

quotes.content: I'm for truth, no matter who tells it. I'm for justice, no matter who it is for or against.
quotes.source: The Autobiography of Malcolm X (1965)
quotes.notes: From a letter written during his pilgrimage to Mecca, quoted in the autobiography's final chapter.

quotes.content: Education is the passport to the future, for tomorrow belongs to those who prepare for it today.
quotes.source: Attributed; primary source not established
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: ubiquitous on aggregator sites but not located in any documented Malcolm X speech, interview, or writing.

quotes.content: Never, ever be afraid to make some noise and get in good trouble, necessary trouble.
quotes.source: Post on Twitter (2018)
quotes.notes: From Lewis's own social-media account; his signature phrase 'good trouble' recurred throughout his speeches and interviews.

quotes.content: Though I may not be here with you, I urge you to answer the highest calling of your heart and stand up for what you truly believe.
quotes.source: Posthumous essay, The New York Times (2020)
quotes.notes: Lewis asked that this essay be published on the day of his funeral, addressed to future generations.

quotes.content: Racism is a grown-up disease, and we must stop using our children to spread it.
quotes.source: Ruby Bridges Foundation statement / rubybridges.com
quotes.notes: The guiding slogan of Bridges's own foundation, stated by her in public remarks and on its official site; minor wording variants exist.
